title: Reverse power rule: rewriting before integrating
description: Find indefinite integrals using the reverse power rule. The integrands in this exercise need to be rewritten before the reverse power rule can be applied.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and indefinite integrals: basic rules and notation: reverse power rule
Reverse power rule: rewriting before integrating

hints:
The integrand is the product of two functions: $x^2$ and $2x-5$. Although it is tempting to take the product of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int f(x)\cdot g(x)\,dx\neq\int f(x)\,dx \cdot \int g(x)\,dx$

Instead, what we should do is expand the parentheses so we get a nice polynomial.

$\displaystyle\int x^2(2x-5)\,dx=\int (2x^3-5x^2)\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int x^2(2x-5)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (2x^3-5x^2)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 2\int x^3\,dx-5\int x^2\,dx
\\\\
&=2\dfrac{x^4}{4}-5\dfrac{x^3}{3}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac12x^4-\dfrac53x^3+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int x^2(2x-5)\,dx=\dfrac12x^4-\dfrac53x^3+C$

The integrand is the product of two functions: $x+6$ and $3x-4$. Although it is tempting to take the product of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int f(x)\cdot g(x)\,dx\neq\int f(x)\,dx \cdot \int g(x)\,dx$

Instead, what we should do is expand the parentheses so we get a nice polynomial.

$\displaystyle\int (x+6)(3x-4)\,dx=\int (3x^2+14x-24)\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (x+6)(3x-4)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (3x^2+14x-24)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 3\int x^{2}\,dx +14\int x\,dx -24\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=3\dfrac{x^3}{3} +14\dfrac{x^2}{2} -24\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=x^3 +7 x^2 -24 x +C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (x+6)(3x-4)\,dx=x^3 +7 x^2 -24 x +C$

The integrand is the product of two functions: $3x$ and $6-x^2$. Although it is tempting to take the product of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int f(x)\cdot g(x)\,dx\neq\int f(x)\,dx \cdot \int g(x)\,dx$

Instead, what we should do is expand the parentheses so we get a nice polynomial.

$\displaystyle\int 3x(6-x^2)\,dx=\int (18x-3x^3)\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int 3x(6-x^2)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (18x-3x^3)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 18\int x\,dx -3\int x^{3}\,dx
\\\\
&=18\dfrac{x^2}{2} -3\dfrac{x^4}{4}+C
\\\\
&=9 x^2 -\dfrac{3}{4} x^4   +C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int 3x(6-x^2)\,dx=9 x^2 -\dfrac{3}{4} x^4   +C$

The integrand is the product of two functions: $2x+1$ and $2x-1$. Although it is tempting to take the product of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int f(x)\cdot g(x)\,dx\neq\int f(x)\,dx \cdot \int g(x)\,dx$

Instead, what we should do is expand the parentheses so we get a nice polynomial.

$\displaystyle\int (2x+1)(2x-1)\,dx=\int (4x^2-1)\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int (2x+1)(2x-1)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (4x^2-1)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 4\int x^{2}\,dx -\int 1\,dx
\\\\
&=4\dfrac{x^3}{3} -\dfrac{x^1}{1} \dfrac{}{}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{4}{3} x^3 -x   +C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (2x+1)(2x-1)\,dx=\dfrac{4}{3} x^3 -x   +C$

The integrand is the quotient of two functions: $x^3+2x^2-4$ and $x^2$. Although it is tempting to take the quotient of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\,dx\neq\dfrac{\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx}{\displaystyle\int g(x)\,dx}$

Instead, what we should do is divide each term in the numerator by the denominator.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{x^3+2x^2-4}{x^2}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{x^3}{x^2}+\dfrac{2x^2}{x^2}-\dfrac{4}{x^2}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (x+2-4x^{-2})\,dx
\end{align}$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{x^3+2x^2-4}{x^2}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (x+2-4x^{-2})\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int x\,dx+2\int 1\,dx-4\int x^{-2}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+2\dfrac{x^1}{1}-4\dfrac{x^{-1}}{-1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac12x^2+2x+\dfrac{4}{x}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{x^3+2x^2-4}{x^2}\right)dx=\dfrac12x^2+2x+\dfrac{4}{x}+C$

The integrand is the quotient of two functions: $3 x^{3} -4 x -2 $ and $x^3$. Although it is tempting to take the quotient of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\,dx\neq\dfrac{\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx}{\displaystyle\int g(x)\,dx}$

Instead, what we should do is divide each term in the numerator by the denominator.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{3 x^{3} -4 x -2 }{x^3}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{3 x^{3}}{x^3}-\dfrac{4 x}{x^3}-\dfrac{2 }{x^3}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (3  -4 x^{-2} -2 x^{-3})\,dx
\end{align}$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{3 x^{3} -4 x -2 }{x^3}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (3  -4 x^{-2} -2 x^{-3})\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 3\int 1\,dx -4\int x^{-2}\,dx -2\int x^{-3}\,dx
\\\\
&=3\dfrac{x^1}{1} -4\dfrac{x^{-1}}{-1} -2\dfrac{x^{-2}}{-2}+C
\\\\
&=3 x + \dfrac{4}{x} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{3 x^{3} -4 x -2 }{x^3}\right)dx=3 x + \dfrac{4}{x} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}+C$

The integrand is the quotient of two functions: $- x^{2} + x$ and $x^4$. Although it is tempting to take the quotient of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\,dx\neq\dfrac{\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx}{\displaystyle\int g(x)\,dx}$

Instead, what we should do is divide each term in the numerator by the denominator.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{- x^{2} + x}{x^4}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(-\dfrac{x^{2}}{x^4}+\dfrac{x}{x^4}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (- x^{-2} + x^{-3} )\,dx
\end{align}$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{- x^{2} + x}{x^4}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (- x^{-2} + x^{-3})\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle -\int x^{-2}\,dx +\int x^{-3}\,dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{x^{-1}}{-1} +\dfrac{x^{-2}}{-2} +C
\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{x} -\dfrac{1}{2x^2} +C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{- x^{2} + x + }{x^4}\right)dx= \dfrac{1}{x} -\dfrac{1}{2x^2} +C$

The integrand is the quotient of two functions: $2 x^{3} +5 x^{2} -2 x$ and $x$. Although it is tempting to take the quotient of their integrals, this would not work.

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\,dx\neq\dfrac{\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx}{\displaystyle\int g(x)\,dx}$

Instead, what we should do is divide each term in the numerator by the denominator.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{2 x^{3} +5 x^{2} -2 x}{x}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{2 x^{3}}{x}+\dfrac{5 x^{2}}{x}-\dfrac{2 x}{x}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (2 x^{2} +5 x -2 )\,dx
\end{align}$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule, the sum rule, and the constant multiple rule for indefinite integrals.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{2 x^{3} +5 x^{2} -2 x}{x}\right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int (2 x^{2} +5 x -2 )\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle 2\int x^{2}\,dx +5\int x\,dx -2\int1 \,dx
\\\\
&=2\dfrac{x^3}{3} +5\dfrac{x^2}{2} -2\dfrac{x^1}{1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3} x^3 +\dfrac{5}{2} x^2 -2 x+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \left( \dfrac{2 x^{3} +5 x^{2} -2 x}{x}\right)dx=\dfrac{2}{3} x^3 +\dfrac{5}{2} x^2 -2 x+C$

At first it might seem as if we can't apply any rule we've learned to find the indefinite integral of a radical function.

However, remember that any radical can be rewritten as a rational power.

$\displaystyle\int \sqrt[3]{x^7}\,dx=\int x^{^{\frac73}}\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\int \sqrt[3]{x^7}\,dx&=\displaystyle\int x^{^{\frac73}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x^{^{\frac73+1}}}{\dfrac73+1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}x^{^{\frac{10}{3}}}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \sqrt[3]{x^7}\,dx=\dfrac{3}{10}x^{^{\frac{10}{3}}}+C$

At first it might seem as if we can't apply any rule we've learned to find the indefinite integral of a radical function.

However, remember that any radical can be rewritten as a rational power.

$\displaystyle\int \sqrt[5]{x^2}\,dx=\int x^{^{\frac25}}\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\int \sqrt[5]{x^2}\,dx&=\displaystyle\int x^{^{\frac{2}{5}}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x^{^{\frac{2}{5}+1}}}{\dfrac{2}{5}+1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{7} x^{^{\frac{7}{5}}}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int \sqrt[5]{x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{5}{7} x^{^{\frac{7}{5}}}+C$

At first it might seem as if we can't apply any rule we've learned to find the indefinite integral of a radical function.

However, remember that any radical can be rewritten as a rational power.

$\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[3]{x})^4\,dx=\int x^{^{\frac{4}{3}}}\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[3]{x})^4\,dx&=\displaystyle\int x^{^{\frac{4}{3}}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x^{^{\frac{4}{3}+1}}}{\dfrac{4}{3}+1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{7} x^{^{\frac{7}{3}}}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[3]{x})^4\,dx=\dfrac{3}{7} x^{^{\frac{7}{3}}}+C$

At first it might seem as if we can't apply any rule we've learned to find the indefinite integral of a radical function.

However, remember that any radical can be rewritten as a rational power.

$\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[6]{x})^3\,dx=\int x^{^{\frac36}}\,dx$

Now we can integrate using the reverse power rule:

$\displaystyle\int x^n\,dx=\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[6]{x})^3\,dx&=\displaystyle\int x^{^{\frac{3}{6}}}\,dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac{x^{^{\frac{3}{6}+1}}}{\dfrac{3}{6}+1}+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3} x^{^{\frac{3}{2}}}+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int (\sqrt[6]{x})^3\,dx=\dfrac{2}{3} x^{^{\frac{3}{2}}}+C$
